The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 10.1 SYMBOLS OF ELECTRIC, COMPONENTS, SCIENCE 110, ELECTRIC CURRENT AND ITS EFFECTS 111. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Respiration in Organisms is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that electric Current and its Effects 10 Y ou might have tried the game ‘How steady is your hand?’ suggested in Chapter 9 of Class VI.

The text explains that paheli and Boojho had also set up the game by connecting an electric circuit as suggested in Class VI.

The text explains that they had lots of fun trying it out with their families and friends.

The text explains that they enjoyed it so much that they decided to suggest it to a cousin of theirs who stayed in a different town.

The text explains that so, Paheli made a neat drawing showing how the various electric components were to be connected (Fig.10.1).

The text explains that 10.1 Setup to check how steady your hand is Can you draw this circuit conveniently?

The text explains that it made Boojho wonder if there was an easier way to represent these electric components.

The text explains that 10.1 SYMBOLS OF ELECTRIC COMPONENTS Some common electric components can be represented by symbols.

The text explains that in Table 10.1, some electric components and their symbols are shown.

The text explains that you may come across different symbols for these components in different books.

The text explains that however, in this book, we shall be using the symbols shown here.

The text explains that in the symbol for the electric cell, notice that there is a longer line and a shorter but thicker parallel line.

The text explains that do you recall that an electric cell has a positive terminal and a negative terminal?

The text explains that in the symbol of the electric cell, the longer line represents the positive terminal and the thicker, shorter line represents the negative terminal.

The text explains that for a switch the ‘ON’ position and the ‘OFF’ position are represented by the symbols as shown.

The text explains that the wires used to connect the various components in a circuit are represented by lines.

The text explains that can you help them to find the answer to this question?

The text explains that the fluorescent tube-lights and compact fluorescent lamps (CFLs) are better electricity efficient lighting sources.

The text explains that eLECTRIC CURRENT AND ITS EFFECTS 115 One reason for excessive currents in electrical circuits is the direct touching of wires.

The text explains that another reason for excessive current can be the connection of many devices to a single socket.

The text explains that if by accident the current exceeds this safe limit, the wires an electric bulb gets heated to such a high temperature that it starts glowing.

The text explains that note the direction in which the compass needle is pointing.

The text explains that do you remember the crane about which you read in Chapter 10 of Class VI?

The text explains that we know that the needle of a compass is a tiny magnet, which points in north-south direction.

The text explains that in the process, the hammer at the end of the strip strikes the gong of the bell to produce a sound.

The text explains that this process is repeated in quick succession.

The text explains that move the key to ‘ON’ position and watch carefully in which direction the compass needle gets deflected.

The text explains that note the direction in which the needle gets deflected.

The text explains that however, after reading this chapter Paheli is wondering if the trick was indeed some magic or some science was involved?

The text explains that request an electrician to show you the various types of fuses and MCB and to explain how they work.

The text explains that the magician again muttered some thing and now Boojho could lift the box.

The text explains that the audience, including Paheli and Boojho, were very impressed with the show and felt that the magician had some supernatural powers.

The text explains that can you guess what science might be involved?

The text explains that the credit for the invention of the electric bulb is usually given to Thomas Alva Edison, though others before him had worked on it.

The text explains that he made some 1300 inventions including the electric bulb, gramophone, the motion picture camera and the carbon transmitter, which facilitated the invention of the telephone.

The text explains that make four electromagnets with 20, 40, 60 and 80 turns.

The text explains that connect them one by one to a battery of 2 cells.

The text explains that using an electromagnet, you can make a working model of a railway signal as shown in Fig.10.26.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 10: Respiration in Organisms

prelims_traps: Do not treat 10.1 SYMBOLS OF ELECTRIC and COMPONENTS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Respiration in Organisms; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 10.1 SYMBOLS OF ELECTRIC and COMPONENTS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
